messages.content: What questions can you answer with regional ocean models?

Regional models can be used to bridge the gap between larger scale processes and much smaller scale phenomena – like biogeochemistry. When delving into any regional modeling we need observations. A lot of biochemistry (BGC) observations come from satellite products. Proximity to land can sometimes contaminate the product, and coral reef systems are shallow so they're inherently near land sometimes.
How do you verify regional model output?

We can use datasets like the SODA reanalysis for looking at trends in velocity fields in the equatorial Pacific. Reanlysis products like this operate as pseudo-truth datasets. They still suffer from model biases, but they incorporate observational products and model dynamics to create a continuous and observationally influenced reference dataset.
What can I start to change to modify some of the behavior of the model?

Sometimes weird factors affect disparate variables. Tidal forcing can affect behavior significantly, especially for such small domains. So the fact that if you turned the tides on versus didn't include tides at the boundary forcing, you could have a degree cooler temperatures and that is something that other research have found as well: that internal tide systems can offer relief from some of the worst warm temperatures during heat events.
What types of data sources and observations can you use to run models?

You can use different model output, especially when you need more specific variables (like with BGC). We also use a reanalysis atmosphere product which is very similar to what happened in the world historically. Reanalysis is also from a model but it is our best guess of what the atmosphere was like using observations and data assimilation techniques. Then we use these past ocean states to create hindcasts. The hind cast that we produced was trying to replicate real world ocean conditions along the west coast of the US, and that has important implications for fisheries when we start to think about what can those people who make their livelihoods based on where the fish will be when they will be there. Reanalysis, and these gridded products are important because they have more extensive information as opposed to maybe single point location observations
If I’m working in the northeast pacific, how should I think about the scientific questions in that region and verifying or evaluating the results?

In any region, validating means how well did the model do compared to something we know to be as close to truth as possible. Another factor to consider is that any reanalysis product, any observation, any sampling device has inherent bias and errors, and you have to find a balance of trust between the model results and these “real” results. There is a remote sensing driven reanalysis of the California fisheries, and they have this longstanding array of observations where they’ve  gone out many years taking cruise samples. And so when you start to have that level of data that's been collected, you can then take one of these models that we've run for many years, trying to reproduce those exact same situations, say, okay, how did we do and how can we do better? Sometimes the initial scientific question is more of a drive to have a general purpose model that is accurate over the largest number of metrics possible.
How do I deal with disagreements? Do I want the model and the observations to be pretty much exactly the same?

We want to have something that is reliable and reasonable, but the reality is you will not capture every possible little eddy at every scale. Even at higher resolutions, it might put something in the wrong location because you're limited by your atmosphere resolution and all these other different aspects of what you choose to parameterize or compute. So you want to get the model as close to reality as possible. But also the question is did it do good enough? Did I learn something about the system that is reasonable? So scientific questions where we look at different ecosystem metrics to help us serve users. Instead of trying to know the exact location of an algobloom, we might be focused on providing a general area with a probability of that happening.
What types of variables are you trying to get right to calculate these types of metrics?

We're trying to make sure we get a few key variables right: surface temperature, bottom temperature, and the vertical structure of the water column. These are critical for understanding stratification and the underlying physical processes.We also look closely at currents—how they move and what that implies for connectivity. For example, how plankton or floating organisms might be transported across regions. Salinity is another important factor because it also influences stratification and tells us something about the origin of water masses in the region.From there, we incorporate nutrients like nitrate. All of these work together to inform our understanding of bottom-up production—things like phytoplankton growth, which links directly into the food chain.
So you use these metrics to connect physical and biological systems?

Exactly. We even have plankton categories within our biogeochemical models. Think of plankton as fish food—there are several steps between them, but we can start to trace those connections. Off the Newport Line, for example, we have datasets that break plankton down into different size classes. That gives us a way to evaluate model skill across a wide range of metrics—from temperature and velocity fields to inorganic nutrients and plankton classes.
And how do you actually evaluate model performance across all those variables?

That’s a big part of what we do—compare model output to observational datasets. Some products are data-assimilative, so they’re nudged toward a known state, which helps. Then we assess whether the model has a persistent bias. Is that bias a sign of a fundamental flaw in the model's dynamics? Or could it be something like an eddy that passed over a mooring that the model just didn’t capture at the right place and time?
We’re always asking: is this error systematic, or just noise?
Do you try to match the temporal scale of the observations with your model output?

Absolutely. If we’re comparing a monthly dataset, we need to be using a monthly average from the model. Same thing with annual means. If the model gives daily output, we can’t just take one day and compare it to a long-term mean.

Got it. So, do the features you look for vary by variable? Like, for sea surface temperature, you might care more about long-term averages, but for something like plankton, would you prioritize spatial distribution?

That’s a great point. And yes, often our focus is guided by data availability. For sea surface temperature, satellites give us pretty good global coverage over long timescales, so we can build a comprehensive picture. But for things like plankton and biogeochemistry, the data is much sparser. Some instruments now record video, but in older datasets, plankton size classes were sorted manually. So we often have to work with whatever’s available and let that guide our scientific questions.
For example?

On the Newport Line, we’ve done some work with plankton time series, looking at when food is available for fish and whether it moves the way we expect. Those temporal dynamics are really important. But again, there’s less data to compare to, so we work with what we have.
How do you know whether to trust your model versus the observations?

That’s an ongoing question. We tend to put observation-based products on a pedestal—like, this is the “truth” we need to match. But sometimes they show things that just don’t make sense. For instance, in the Bering Sea, there's a satellite product that shows high chlorophyll levels in January. But that region is covered in sea ice and gets very little sunlight then. There’s no way photosynthesis is happening at those levels in the dark under ice. So in that case, we trust the model more, because we understand the physical limitations better than the observation product might reflect.
So part of our job is not just comparing your model to observations, but also sanity-checking both, right?

Exactly. You need to use your own knowledge to assess whether what you're seeing makes sense. But it’s not just intuition—you can also look at what the literature says. Other researchers have done extensive validation work on these observational products. So we dig into that: What kinds of errors have they reported? What deviations have they seen? What do they recommend being cautious about? By bringing together multiple datasets, you can start to get a clearer picture. Are you just seeing an anomaly in one product? Is there a methodological issue? Or is there genuinely something wrong with the model?
So it becomes about assembling a full picture from different lines of evidence?

Right. Sometimes it comes down to recognizing that, in a particular region, a product might just not be performing well. We deal with this all the time—especially in challenging environments like the Arctic. It’s really hard to collect data there in winter. You can’t safely send out research cruises, and floats might freeze, get crushed by ice, or just disappear. So even the best products rely on sparse observations and have to fill in the gaps. If you're looking at a location with tons of data points, you can trust the result a lot more. But if there's just one point spanning 30 years? It’s better than nothing—but definitely not gospel.
Kind of like reading product reviews—you wouldn't trust a rating if it only had one review.
Exactly.That’s a great analogy. It’s kind of like reading product reviews—you wouldn't trust a rating if it only had one review.
Okay, zooming back out—how do you actually go about improving a model once it's running?

Well, first you have to get it running—and that can be half the battle. It’s common for the model to crash at the beginning, usually due to a bad value in the forcing file or something unstable. Once you get it stable and running, then you can begin digging into the output and looking for issues.
What’s an example of that kind of debugging process?

One case was in the Bering Sea. Our initial runs weren’t maintaining the cold pool that should persist through spring. Bottom temperatures were warming up too fast. When we compared the bottom trawl survey data, we saw that we were getting the 2°C signal right, but we weren’t seeing the really cold water—less than -1°C—that’s supposed to stick around longer.
So I kept poking at it. Something wasn’t sitting right. Eventually, by talking to colleagues, we identified a process that likely wasn’t being represented well on shallow shelves.
What did you change to address that?

The team modified a parameterization related to shear on shallow shelves. It altered how mixing occurred in those regions, reducing it slightly so that stratification was preserved better. I didn’t write the code myself, but the change improved the cold pool behavior. Not perfectly, but noticeably.
Do you have other examples of changes like that?

Yes—another case involved a restratification scheme. In its original form, it used a fixed horizontal length scale to define the distance over which vertical mixing interacted with horizontal gradients. That constant had a big impact on the results. We realized that tweaking it, or ideally making it adaptive, could improve the model’s handling of stratification—especially in transitional seasons.
You were saying earlier that one of the things that helped improve performance was changing how the restratification scheme works?

Yes. Originally, the restratification scheme used a fixed horizontal length scale—a constant value that defined how far stratification would propagate laterally during mixing. But what’s been shown in more recent research is that this length scale should actually vary by season and latitude.
So just setting it to a single value doesn’t capture that variation?

Exactly. If you hard-code a single value, you’re essentially picking something that’s just an average for the domain. That might work okay overall, but it's not ideal for all regions—especially in systems with large spatial variability.
So our colleagues who handle the model code modified it so that the length scale becomes dynamic. Instead of setting it manually, it gets calculated during the run based on physical conditions. That way, it naturally adjusts to the location and time of year.
That sounds like a pretty big improvement.

It really is. Rather than compromising model performance in the far north or south of the domain because we’re using a “best fit” constant, we now calculate something optimal for each part of the domain.
And that’s exactly what you want to do when possible. So often we’re forced to optimize for the mean behavior across the domain—but that can mean sacrificing performance in a critical area. With this update, we actually improved the cold pool representation in the northern domain without degrading the rest of the simulation.
So you define success by whether a change helps in one region without hurting others?

Yes, that’s exactly how I think about it. If we can improve a problematic feature—like bottom temperature bias in the Bering Sea—without introducing new errors elsewhere, then that’s a meaningful improvement.
And how did you know to focus on those variables in the first place? Was it something you were already watching or did it come out of the evaluation process?

We didn’t know beforehand. It came out of collaborative work. We’re part of a larger effort called the Climate, Ecosystems, and Fisheries Initiative, and we work closely with colleagues at the Alaska Fisheries Science Center.
They had already developed an observational index for the Bering Sea cold pool, which is defined as the area of seafloor with bottom temperatures below 2°C—or in some cases below -1°C.
So you used that index with your model output?

Exactly. We tried to reproduce their index using model output instead of cruise data. We sampled the model at the same time and locations as their bottom trawl surveys, like we were a virtual cruise ship. Then we plotted the results and compared them to the observed cold pool plots.
And right away we saw something strange—our model wasn’t showing persistent cold water where it should.
So you brought that issue to your collaborators?

Yep. At our bi-weekly meetings we said, “Hey, something looks off. We’re missing the coldest water even though the air temperature signal seems okay.”
They suggested we examine stratification more carefully and maybe run the model at higher output frequency for a few months to get better diagnostics. Eventually, our colleagues developed a change to the code to address this and submitted it to the main codebase.
That’s a really nice example of collaborative troubleshooting.
Yeah, it really was. The process is very iterative—someone raises a flag, others suggest diagnostics or code changes, and together we work toward a better representation of the system.
Cool. That was great. I’m glad you spent so much time talking through that region—your perspective on it was really special.
Thank you! I'm happy to dive deep into those examples—it helps show how all the pieces of regional modeling come together.

Now we’re going to shift into a bit of a thought experiment. We’d like you to pick a region that you maybe haven’t modeled before and think about what kinds of open questions or processes would be interesting to explore. Hopefully this will also help us surface some of the broader principles you apply when approaching new model development.

Sure. So, if I’m choosing a region just out of personal curiosity, that might be very different from choosing a region that would be easy to model. But one area I find absolutely fascinating is the Coral Triangle in the Indo-Pacific. It’s at the heart of the Indonesian Throughflow and it’s just a remarkable place.
What draws your interest there?

For one, it’s home to the highest diversity of reef-building coral species in the world—over 700 species in some areas. For comparison, I used to do some work in Bermuda, where you might find only 10 to 30 coral species. The scale of biodiversity in the Coral Triangle is just incredible.
It’s also believed that this diversity stems from the region’s unique bathymetry and its response to glacial cycles. When sea levels dropped during glacial maxima, you’d get isolated pockets of water, almost like natural laboratories of evolution. Then, as sea levels rose again, those pockets reconnected and allowed species to mix and spread. It’s a dynamic system, both biologically and geologically.
That’s a great connection between physical and biological systems. What else motivates your interest?

Honestly, they’re beautiful. And biologically fascinating. Corals are part plant, part animal, and part mineral—it’s wild. I also did some work in natural products chemistry during undergrad, so I have a soft spot for biodiversity from a biochemical perspective. These organisms often produce unique chemical compounds, some of which have medical applications—anti-cancer, antimicrobial, you name it. There’s so much we can learn from environments like this.
If you were to set up a model in that region, what would your approach be?

I’d probably place the domain just north of Australia—covering parts of the Great Barrier Reef and extending into the Indonesian Throughflow. And I wouldn’t start from scratch. There’s a ROMS model which covers the Philippines. I’d use that as a base—maybe convert the grid for use in MOM6.
That makes sense. Reuse what works.
Exactly. Don’t reinvent the wheel if a good domain already exists.
And what kinds of features or diagnostics would you focus on?

One major thing would be the Indonesian Throughflow itself. It plays a big role in global climate dynamics by transporting water and heat from the Pacific to the Indian Ocean. So I’d want to assess how well the model represents that flow—how much water mass is actually getting through, and how accurately we're capturing its structure and timing.
In coarse global models, those passageways can end up being represented by just one grid cell, which obviously isn’t ideal. So with a higher-resolution regional model, we can ask whether we’re resolving those narrow straits effectively.
What kinds of data would you use for validation?

It depends on what I’m looking at, but temperature and velocity fields are always a good start. Satellite data would be helpful too, though in this region that’s tricky—the topography is complex and the signal can be noisy or unreliable.
That’s why I’d prioritize collaboration with researchers who actually work in the region. If you’re building a model of the Coral Triangle from thousands of miles away, it’s just good practice—and frankly, more ethical—to engage with scientists on the ground who have access to in situ observations and local knowledge.
So building a scientifically sound model also means building relationships?

Definitely. That’s especially true in places where observational data is hard to come by. You want to work with people who understand the environment and have boots-on-the-ground insight. That makes your model better and makes your research more grounded in reality.
What would the next steps be in actually configuring the model for a region like the Coral Triangle? Where would you start experimenting?

A good starting point would be assembling relevant observational datasets—buoy records, tide gauge data, and any available in situ measurements. These provide a baseline for evaluating model output but only scratch the surface of what’s needed for refinement.
And what about the model inputs themselves? Which would you test first?

Atmospheric forcing would be a logical place to begin. Many existing regional configurations, including those for nearby areas like the Philippines, have used older atmospheric reanalysis products. Replacing those with something like ERA5, which offers higher resolution, could improve representation of wind, rainfall, and other processes—especially in regions with narrow passageways and strong monsoonal influences.
That higher resolution could help with the fine-scale circulation features, right?
Exactly. Better-resolved atmospheric inputs can affect local wind stress, freshwater fluxes, and surface heat exchange—all critical in areas with complex bathymetry and active weather systems.
What about other forcing fields or components? Anything else that would be worth testing early?

Freshwater input from rivers is one area that often gets simplified, particularly in small island systems. While these regions may not have large drainage basins, they do receive substantial rainfall. Improved representation of river inflows—especially during the monsoon season—can influence coastal salinity and nutrient delivery, and might be more important than previously assumed.
Would biogeochemistry be included at this stage?

It’s something that could be layered in as the physical model stabilizes. There’s been growing interest in better representing benthic biogeochemical fluxes, especially over shelves and reef systems. These benthic exchanges have often been overlooked in global models, but they're crucial for accuracy in regions like the Coral Triangle, where calcium carbonate-producing organisms actively influence local chemistry.
Are there ways to validate those processes if included?

Yes—field programs in the region sometimes deploy moored buoys to collect time series of alkalinity and dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC). These observations could be used to compare against model output, offering insight into whether the simulated carbonate system is behaving realistically.
Would the goal ultimately be to resolve things like carbon fluxes across the reef?

That would be the long-term objective. Once the physical circulation is well resolved, the model could be used to explore how reef systems contribute to local and regional carbon cycling. Questions could include: Are certain coral species or reef types more influential? Does biodiversity matter for modulating carbonate chemistry? In some areas, seagrass is being planted to draw down CO₂—should that process be explicitly modeled, or is it too localized to matter at this scale?
So essentially, physical realism is the prerequisite before diving into any of that?

Correct. Physical dynamics govern how nutrients, carbon, and other tracers are transported. If the circulation isn’t behaving as expected, then any conclusions drawn about biogeochemical behavior would be unreliable.
Let’s build on the Coral Triangle example. Say a regional model is being set up in that area—what would be the initial experiments or boundary conditions to test?

The process would begin with assembling observational datasets—buoy records, tide gauge data, and any available in situ measurements—to serve as baseline references. These provide critical context but only begin to address the system’s complexity.
A key early focus would be atmospheric forcing. Many previous regional setups, including those near the Philippines, used older reanalysis products. By contrast, newer reanalysis products like ERA5 offer significantly improved spatial and temporal resolution, allowing finer-scale atmospheric processes to be represented. This is especially important in regions with complex coastal geometries and narrow passageways, such as the Coral Triangle, where monsoonal winds and precipitation play a dominant role in surface forcing.
Beyond atmospheric forcing, what other model components would benefit from refinement?

River inflow representation is often overlooked in small island systems. While the land area may be limited, regions like the Coral Triangle receive significant rainfall, especially during monsoon seasons. Accurately modeling the freshwater inputs from rivers can impact coastal salinity gradients and nutrient transport.
Biogeochemical processes are another area of interest. Recent modeling efforts have incorporated more detailed benthic fluxes, especially in reef environments. These systems are shaped by calcium carbonate production and dissolution, meaning local carbonate chemistry can shift significantly. Capturing those dynamics requires realistic representation of benthic exchanges.
Field campaigns that collect alkalinity and dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC) measurements—often using buoys with extended sampling periods—offer valuable validation opportunities. Comparing model outputs against these datasets can provide insight into how well carbonate dynamics are captured in situ.
So in a case like this, would the goal be to eventually resolve full carbon and nutrient cycling?

That would be the trajectory. Achieving credible biogeochemical behavior requires accurate physical dynamics as a foundation—especially flow pathways, mixing, and stratification. Without this, the movement and transformation of nutrients and carbon would not be realistic.
Once physical processes are sufficiently resolved, the model could be used to explore questions about carbon fluxes associated with reef systems. For example: To what extent do coral species composition and biodiversity impact local carbon cycling? How significant are seagrass beds in drawing down CO₂, and does this process need to be explicitly modeled in regional configurations? These questions could be approached once the transport and mixing framework is deemed robust.

In another interview, a colleague working on a Caribbean model described modifying bathymetry in narrow passages to improve flow representation. When working with complicated topography like the Coral Triangle, how concerning are unphysical bathymetric changes—especially with biogeochemistry involved?

This is a common challenge in regional modeling. In early development stages, slight bathymetric adjustments are often made to achieve realistic flow fields, particularly in straits and narrow passages where coarse resolution may underrepresent true geometry.
For systems like the Indonesian Throughflow, this is critical. These channels play a central role in inter-ocean heat and mass exchange. If bathymetry prevents adequate flow, then downstream fields—such as temperature, salinity, nutrients, and carbon—will be misrepresented.
While maintaining observational fidelity is always preferred, realistic flow dynamics are essential to achieving physically consistent results. Any changes should be assessed carefully to ensure they do not introduce unrealistic habitat conditions or disrupt local ecosystems. Ultimately, flow realism is not just a physical concern—it directly governs the accuracy of all subsequent ecological and biogeochemical interactions in the model.
